raw_response:
## Overall Assessment

Governance controls supporting **CC1.1 — commitment to integrity and ethical values** are **not fully operating effectively** for the 2025 audit period based on the evidence provided. The main issues are missed annual policy reviews, incomplete Security Steering Committee oversight, inconsistent reporting of required metrics, and lack of documented follow-up on policy exceptions and underperforming compliance metrics.

| # | Finding Title | Related Section / Evidence | Why It’s a Gap | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Information Security Policy annual review was missed | **Information Security Policy Section 4.2 — Policy Review Cycle**; Policy Review Register; Q2 SSC minutes | The policy requires all Tier 1 and Tier 2 policies to be reviewed annually by the CISO and approved by the Security Steering Committee. POL-ISEC-002 was due for review on **April 15, 2025**, but the register states: “No review conducted in 2025” and shows it **199 days overdue**. Q2 minutes noted the Tier 1 policy review was pending with a June 30 due date, but Q3 minutes do not show completion or follow-up. | **High** |
| 2 | Annual policy review and approval process is not consistently operating | **Information Security Policy Section 4.2**; Policy Review Register | Several policies appear to have been reviewed by functional owners, but there is no evidence that the **CISO reviewed** and the **Security Steering Committee approved** all Tier 1 and Tier 2 policies as required. For example, Access Control and Encryption were reviewed by Security Engineering, Code of Conduct by HR, and Vendor Risk by GRC, but the register does not evidence SSC approval. | **Medium** |
| 3 | Vulnerability Management Standard review is overdue | **Information Security Policy Section 4.2**; Policy Review Register | POL-VUL-001 was due for annual review on **September 15, 2025** and is listed as **49 days overdue**. The note says the review was deferred due to SOC 2 audit preparation. Deferring a required governance activity because of audit preparation indicates the control is not operating as designed. | **Medium** |
| 4 | Policy review register contains inconsistent status information | **Information Security Policy Section 4.2**; Policy Review Register | The register shows some policies as “Completed” even when they are overdue or still in progress. Examples: POL-ISEC-002 is marked “Completed” but is 199 days overdue; POL-VUL-001 is marked “Completed” but also listed as overdue; POL-BCP-001 is marked “Completed” while the note says “Review in progress.” This weakens the reliability of the register as the control evidence. | **Low** |
| 5 | Security Steering Committee quarterly operation not fully evidenced | **Information Security Policy Section 3.1 — Security Steering Committee**; SSC minutes | The policy requires the Security Steering Committee to meet quarterly. Only Q2 and Q3 2025 meeting minutes were provided. There is no evidence of a Q1 2025 meeting, and no Q4 2025 minutes were provided, even though the next meeting was scheduled for October 22, 2025. For a January 1–December 31 Type II period, evidence should demonstrate operation across all quarters. | **Medium** |
| 6 | Required security metrics were not fully reviewed by the committee | **Information Security Policy Section 6 — Security Metrics**; Q2 and Q3 SSC minutes; Q3 Security Metrics Report | The policy requires the CISO to report five metrics quarterly: MTTD, MTTR, vulnerability remediation compliance, training completion, and policy exceptions. Q2 and Q3 minutes primarily document discussion of MTTD and MTTR. Q2 minutes separately discuss training, but there is no documented review of vulnerability remediation or policy exceptions. Q3 minutes explicitly state there was “no further discussion” on vulnerability remediation, training completion, or policy exceptions. | **Medium** |
| 7 | Off-target Q3 security metrics lack documented governance follow-up | **Information Security Policy Section 6**; Q3 Security Metrics Report; SSC minutes | The Q3 metrics report shows several off-target or concerning results: critical vulnerability remediation at **75% vs. 100% target**, high vulnerability remediation at **70.6% vs. 95% target**, security awareness completion at **94.6% vs. 95% target**, and one expired policy exception without renewal or remediation. No Q4 committee minutes or action plan evidence was provided to show these issues were reviewed, accepted, or remediated. | **High** |
| 8 | Vulnerability remediation requirements were not met | **Information Security Policy Section 5.3 — Vulnerability Management**; Q3 Security Metrics Report | The policy requires critical vulnerabilities to be remediated within **72 hours** and high vulnerabilities within **30 days**. In Q3, two critical vulnerabilities affecting the payment processing module were not remediated within the 72-hour window, and 10 high vulnerabilities were overdue. Although compensating WAF controls were deployed, the required remediation SLA was not met. | **High** |
| 9 | Policy exception governance does not align with policy authority limits | **Information Security Policy Section 3.2 — CISO Authority**; Q3 Security Metrics Report | The policy states the CISO may approve security exceptions “up to 90 days.” However, multiple exceptions appear to have approval periods longer than 90 days, including EXC-2025-003 from June 10 to December 10 and EXC-2025-004 from July 1 to January 1. Additionally, EXC-2025-001 expired on August 15 without renewal or remediation. This indicates exceptions are not being governed according to policy. | **Medium** |
| 10 | Security awareness training target was missed, including executive non-completion | **Q3 Security Metrics Report — Metric 4**; CC1.1 tone-at-the-top expectation | Overall Q3 security awareness completion was **94.6%**, slightly below the 95% target. More notably, executive completion was only **75%**, with the CTO and CFO overdue. For CC1.1, executive participation is important because it demonstrates tone at the top and commitment to ethical and secure behavior. | **Medium** |
| 11 | Evidence of policy communication and distribution is missing | **Information Security Policy Section 4.3 — Policy Distribution** | The policy requires approved policies to be published on the intranet and employees to be notified of policy changes by email. No intranet publication evidence, change notification emails, or employee acknowledgement records were provided. This is especially relevant for demonstrating that expectations for integrity, ethical conduct, and security responsibilities are communicated. | **Low** |
| 12 | Code of Conduct evidence is insufficient for CC1.1 readiness | Policy Review Register; **CC1.1** | The register shows the Code of Conduct was refreshed on January 5, 2025, but the actual Code of Conduct, employee acknowledgement records, ethics training, reporting hotline procedures, or disciplinary enforcement evidence were not provided. For CC1.1, a reviewed Code of Conduct alone is not enough to demonstrate that integrity and ethical values are communicated and reinforced across the company. | **Medium** |
